ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03315873 (muesli) is the closest defensible match for a wholegrain cereal mixed with dried fruits and nuts. An exact FoodOn code for 'wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts' as a generic category was not identified; 'closest_parent' match type used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E (muesli and similar cereal products) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for this generic wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts product. Exact FoodEx2 code for this specific generic formulation not available; 'closest_parent' match type documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts as a generic food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts is approximately 45g (about 3–4 tablespoons dry), providing roughly 187 kcal, 3.7g protein, 5.5g fat, and 30.9g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts' (cr_id: F05050028). All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central muesli reference (USDA SR Legacy item 08120) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Muesli, natural, no added sugar — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry for 'Wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts' as a generic HPB category was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest analogous entry is muesli with mixed dried fruits and nuts (GI approximately 56–66, mean ~66 used here). GI of 66 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate for a wholegrain cereal with dried fruits and added sugar, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database muesli entries. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (66 × 30.9) / 100 = 20.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (21.5g/100g) with low potassium (59mg/100g) and moderate sodium (126mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: The high sugar load from dried fruits and added sugar promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, which can elevate blood pressure; patients with hypertension should choose unsweetened wholegrain muesli variants and pair with fresh fruit to improve potassium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 21.5g/100g exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate is 68.6g/100g with a medium-to-high GI of 66
type2_diabetes.guidance: The concentrated sugar from dried fruits and added sugar will cause significant post-meal glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product or strictly limit portion size to 20–25g, and should instead select no-added-sugar muesli with whole nuts and seeds for a lower-GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat (12.3g/100g) with low saturated fat (~1.8g/100g); high sugar (21.5g/100g) raises triglyceride risk; fibre (6.1g/100g) and nuts provide cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The wholegrain base and nut content provide beneficial unsaturated fats and soluble fibre that support LDL reduction, but the high sugar content from dried fruits and added sugar may elevate triglycerides; patients with cardiovascular disease should choose unsweetened versions and monitor portion size to limit sugar-driven dyslipidaemia.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potential anti-inflammatory benefit from nuts (omega-3 ALA if walnuts present) and fibre (6.1g/100g); offset by pro-inflammatory high sugar (21.5g/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: If the nut component includes walnuts or flaxseed, the omega-3 ALA and vitamin E content provide anti-inflammatory benefit and the fibre supports gut microbiome immune modulation; however, the high sugar content from dried fruits has pro-inflammatory potential and unsweetened versions are strongly preferred for patients with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts is a ready-to-eat or bowl-served breakfast cereal composed of a wholegrain base — typically rolled oats, wheat flakes, or a mixed-grain blend — combined with dried fruits such as raisins, sultanas, or cranberries, and nuts such as almonds, walnuts, or cashews. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or snack food, served dry or with milk, yogurt, or plant-based alternatives. The texture is crunchy to chewy depending on the grain and fruit ratio, with a naturally sweet flavour from the dried fruit component. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 416 kcal, 8.3g protein, 12.3g fat (predominantly unsaturated from nuts), 68.6g carbohydrate, 21.5g sugar, 6.1g dietary fibre, 126mg sodium, 94mg calcium, and 4.4mg iron. The high fibre content from wholegrains supports digestive health and gut microbiome diversity. However, the sugar content of 21.5g/100g — largely from concentrated dried fruits and potentially added sugar — is a significant clinical concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension, as it exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling and may drive post-meal glucose spikes and insulin-mediated sodium retention. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 66 (medium-high), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 20 per standard 45g serving. The wholegrain base and nut content provide cardioprotective unsaturated fats and soluble beta-glucan fibre. For clinical populations, unsweetened muesli variants with whole nuts and seeds and no added sugar are strongly preferred over sweetened or dried-fruit-heavy formulations.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05050028, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat is estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy muesli reference and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database muesli entries and is clearly documented as such.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for muesli (USDA NDB 08120 / similar muesli reference) as the HPB Foodo database does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This is an approximate value and should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, and moisture data for this generic wholegrain cereal with fruits or nuts category are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact formulation as of 2026. 3. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database muesli entries; the exact GI for this specific HPB generic formulation has not been independently measured. 4. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is an international/Western generic cereal product. Multilingual local-name fields are therefore not applicable and are set to null. No G6 multilingual naming obligation applies. 5. Cholesterol is reported at 0.28mg/100g per HPB source; this trace level is consistent with a predominantly plant-based product where trace cholesterol may arise from minor dairy or animal-derived processing aids. 6. The food is classified as 'processed' due to the industrial mixing, possible toasting, and addition of dried fruits and nuts to a wholegrain base.
